title: What changes and what does not
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted everything instead of fixing it.
why.untouched: script.js is exactly as it was.
hint2.untouched: The code runs and does not break. That is exactly the problem — look at the last two numbers.
why.not-closed: A tag in index.html is open and never closed.
hint2.not-closed: index.html is not part of this lesson.
why.not-linked: The script never reaches the page.
why.crashed: Your code stopped with an error before it reached the end.
hint2.crashed: Look at the Console panel below.
hint3.crashed: TypeError: Assignment to constant variable means you are trying to change something declared with const. That is exactly what const does — it refuses.
why.frozen: Your code never stops. It loops forever and was cut off.
why.reassign-left: The line price = 4 is still there. The price changes halfway through and the last sum lies.
hint2.reassign-left: That line should never have existed. Delete it.
hint3.reassign-left: The first two lines calculate with a price of 3. After price = 4 the rest calculate with 4. Nobody complains — the numbers are simply different.
hint2.sold-not-const: The number of coffees sold does not change in this code. So it should not be able to.
hint3.sold-not-const: const cups = 12; creates a name that cannot point at anything else. Try it and the code breaks on the spot instead of quietly calculating with a wrong value.
hint2.price-not-const: The price should not change either. Make it const and see what happens.
why.vat-const: vat is declared with const and changed on the next line. That cannot work.
hint2.vat-const: Not everything should be const. The tax is calculated later — it changes on purpose.
hint3.vat-const: The rule is not const always. The rule is const until something proves it has to change.
why.no-sold: There is no name sold holding 1247.
why.no-price: There is no name price holding 3.
why.no-vat: The tax is not right. It should be twenty percent of the total.
hint2.no-vat: vat is calculated from sold and price. If the price changed in between, the number will be different.
why.wrong-last: The last number is wrong. It is being calculated with the wrong price.
hint2.wrong-last: 1247 times 3 minus 400 is 3341. Anything else means the price is 4 somewhere.
why.lost-p: A line has disappeared from the page.
blocks.0.text: Look at script.js. The code runs. Nothing breaks, the console is clean, all four numbers come out.
blocks.1.text: And two of them are wrong.
blocks.3.text: Somewhere in the middle the price changes from 3 to 4. The first two sums use the old price, the last one uses the new one. No error, no warning. Just numbers that do not add up.
blocks.4.text: This is the most common kind of bug in programming, and it has nothing to do with syntax. The code is correct. What it means is not.
blocks.6.text: let creates a name that can point at something else. const creates one that cannot. Try to change it and the code stops right there.
blocks.8.text: TypeError: Assignment to constant variable. An error that appears immediately and points at the exact line.
blocks.9.text: Compare that with the alternative: a silent change you discover two months later, because somebody tells you the report does not add up.
blocks.11.text: const is the sensible default for everything. You write let only when the value genuinely has to change — a loop counter, a running total, something that depends on what the user picks.
blocks.12.text: The opposite rule — let everywhere, just in case — is exactly what allowed the bug above to exist.
blocks.14.text: Delete the line that changes the price. Then make sold and price constants.
blocks.15.text: vat stays let — it is calculated after it is created and has to be able to change. Make it const too and the code will break, and you will see why.
